does: This paper conducts the first systematic cross-lingual, multimodal red-teaming study of jailbreak vulnerabilities across four frontier MLLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5, Pixtral Large, and Qwen Omni) by administering 363 diverse adversarial prompt scenarios in both US English and Mexican Spanish across text-only and multimodal conditions. Using Bayesian mixed-effects analyses on 52,272 harm ratings from matched native-speaker annotator panels, the work reveals that language does not scale vulnerability uniformly: linguistic framing attacks like role-play become substantially less effective under Spanish prompting while visually explicit multimodal attacks become more effective, demonstrating a dissociation that directly implicates the prompt-language interface as a mechanistic factor in alignment failures rather than annotator bias or global model behavior differences.
connects: This work extends the growing body of adversarial red-teaming research on large language models and multimodal systems by introducing systematic cross-lingual analysis, building on prior jailbreak studies that typically examine single-language vulnerabilities. It responds to limitations in earlier multimodal safety research that has largely focused on English-only evaluation and ignored how linguistic and visual modalities interact in shaping attack surfaces. The paper connects to broader mechanistic interpretability work seeking to understand alignment failures by revealing how different linguistic framings expose distinct vulnerability patterns, while also building on multimodal alignment research that has documented how vision and language components can have asymmetric safety properties.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing language-aware alignment and safety techniques that account for cross-lingual vulnerability asymmetries in frontier MLLMs. It enables more targeted defense strategies that recognize different attack vectors become more or less effective depending on linguistic and modal framing. The finding that multimodal attacks can compensate for linguistic robustness suggests future work on integrated vision-language defense mechanisms rather than modality-agnostic approaches. This research also motivates investigation into the mechanistic roots of language-dependent vulnerability patterns, potentially informing how MLLMs encode safety training differently across languages and modalities, and could guide development of more equitable global safety standards that don't rely on English-centric vulnerability assumptions.
